title: ANTH 102 - Introduction to the Anthropology of Language
description: Overview of anthropological study of language structure, use and change. Topics include language emergence, verbal arts and technologies, literacy, linguistic relativity, fieldwork design features and diverse social and historical contexts.

title: ANTH 103 - Biological Anthropology
description: Introduction to human evolution and adaptation through scientific principles, evolutionary theory, genetic inheritance, nonhuman primates, fossil record, forensics, and evolutionary medicine. Not intended for most science/natural resource majors.

title: ANTH 104 - Cultural Anthropology
description: World’s diverse cultures. Richness of human life in different times and places. Multicultural nature of today’s world.

title: ANTH 105 - Archaeology and World Prehistory
description: Explore the discipline of archaeology, including its history, materials, and methods; trace the many paths of cultural evolution around the world and across time as reconstructed from the archaeological record.

title: ANTH 210 - Introduction to the Anthropology Major
description: Explore program requirements, including career trajectories and emphasis selection. Develop a plan for successful completion of the degree. Connect with department community and learn about the department resources and facilities.

title: ANTH 310W - Critical Histories of Culture, Race, and Science
description: Examines counter-lineages and counter-histories of anthropological thought across time through a multivocality of diverse thinkers to interrogate knowledge production of fraught concepts such as race, primitiveness, and self/other.

title: ANTH 410 - Anthropology Capstone
description: Capstone seminar on contemporary anthropological theory designed to prepare students for an academic or applied career using their anthropology degree. Final course projects may reflect students’ sub-disciplinary interests.

title: ANTH 318 - Ethnography
description: Problems and techniques of describing culture and representing the “other.” Critical look at the process and politics of descriptions anthropologists craft.

title: ANTH 330 - Method and Theory in Biological Anthropology
description: Introduces major research areas within biological anthropology, including their fundamental theories, scientific concepts, and methods of data collection and analysis.

title: ANTH 350 - Method and Theory in Archaeology
description: Roles of theory and scientific method in reconstructing past cultures, culture process, and change.

title: ANTH 482 - Anthropology Internship
description: Engages students at Cal Poly Humboldt anthropology research and teaching facilities, local agencies, community organizations, and other institutions to enhance students’ knowledge of applied anthropology contexts, methodologies, institutional cultures, and work environments.

title: ANTH 485 - Senior Seminar
description: Advanced topics with relevance for the entire anthropology discipline. Check with faculty for course content and prerequisites.

title: NAS 101 - Traditional Ecological Knowledge and Place Based Learning
description: Introduction to traditional ecological knowledge (TEK) and ethical engagement of place-based learning. Apply critical thinking skills to responsible incorporation of Indigenous knowledges and explore how critical and creative thinking occurs within Indigenous contexts. Engage with informed present-day issues and explore how TEK can inform critical and creative thinking and problem-solving skills across disciplines.

title: NAS 104 - Introduction to Native American Studies
description: Origins and development of content/method in Native American studies. Contrast the field with adjoining and contributing disciplines (anthropology, history, sociology, and humanities).

title: NAS 107 - Introduction to California Indian Peoples and Places
description: Introduction to California Indian tribes and place-based learning exploring contemporary political, social, environmental issues and representations through California Indian perspectives and knowledges. A place-based approach to anti-racist and anti-colonial movements through community-centered learning and a foundational overview of California Indian tribes. Topics can include but are not limited to: Tribal citizenship, Self-determination, Decolonization, Sovereignty, and Settler Colonialism.

title: NAS 200 - Indigenous Peoples in U.S. History
description: How U.S. policies governing tribes are shaped by societal views of Indigenous Peoples. Analyze documents following history of federal Indian policy. Interactions among diverse Native, European, African, and Asian Americans. Meets requirement in U.S. history established by California legislature.

title: NAS 306 - Indigenous Peoples of the Americas
description: Traditional cultures, historical development, and contemporary social and political situations of Indigenous peoples of South, Central, and North America, with an emphasis on Latin America (Abya Yala).

title: NAS 361 - Tribal Sovereignty, Tribal Citizens
description: Comprehensive review of NA civics and dual role of tribal citizenship in the US. Topics: tribal governance, tribal justice systems, Indian-White relations, education, religious conflict, community development.

title: NAS 480 - Selected Topics in Native American Studies
description: Special topic, problem area, or field research. Repeatable with different topics.

title: ANTH 305 - Human Evolutionary Health
description: How the biology, behavior, diet, strategies, and experiences of our ancestors are reflected in and affect us today; examine human biocultural diversity within the context of evolutionary history.

title: ANTH 331 - Paleoanthropology
description: Evolutionary theory, the behavior, ecology, and morphology of human ancestors, and the emergence of our species. This course is framed by the major debates and rivalries within the discipline.

title: ANTH 332 - Skeletal Biology and Forensics
description: Intensive study of human osteology and skeletal biology, including techniques for creating biological profiles and estimating age, sex, stature, trauma, and disease; application to legal investigations and human rights.

title: ANTH 333 - Primate Adaptation and Evolution
description: Nonhuman primate evolution and adaptation; what makes primates unique mammals; models for big evolutionary questions; systematics; anatomy; behavioral ecology and strategies of extant primates; biogeography; primate origins; adaptive radiation; fossils.

title: ANTH 334 - Anthropology, Ecology and Conservation
description: Interrelationships between humans and the environment, with an eye to conservation. Topics such as: ecology of human and nonhuman primate habitats; biogeography, biodiversity; biotic interactions; globalization; population growth; climate change.

title: ANTH 339 - Special Topics in Biological Anthropology
description: Seminars on topics such as: advanced bioanth methods, evolutionary theory, human osteology, functional/evolutionary anatomy, primate evolution, ecology, conservation, nutrition, medical anthropology, epidemiology, epigenetics, zooarchaeology, taphonomy and bioarchaeology.

title: BIOL 105 - Principles of Biology
description: Fundamental processes of life. Structure and function of cells, genetics, evolution, and ecology.

title: BOT 105 - General Botany
description: Structure, function, reproduction, life cycles, and phylogenetic relationships of major plant groups. Relationships of plants to other organisms and to human activities.

title: CHEM 107 - Fundamentals of Chemistry
description: Terminal course. Fundamental concepts and applications of general and inorganic chemistry.

title: CHEM 109 - General Chemistry I
description: Fundamental concepts: chemical foundations, stoichiometry, chemical reactions, gases, thermochemistry, atomic theory, bonding, liquids, solutions. For students in science, engineering, and related majors.

title: FOR 210 - Forest Measurements and Biometry
description: Surveying including angle and distance measurement, leveling and traverse. Public land survey. Topographic map reading and construction. Tree and forest measurements under field conditions. Forest sampling theory. Introductory statistical analysis of forest measurements.

title: MATH 109 - Calculus I
description: Limits, continuity, derivatives, integrals, and their applications.

title: NAS 305 - Native American Health
description: Introduction to Native American public health issues and contributing factors (like environmental justice and historical trauma); social determinants of health; cultural dimensions of diagnosis and best practices for promoting health in Native American communities.

title: NAS 308 - Indigenous Ethnobotany
description: Rooted in Indigenous research paradigms and land-based pedagogies. Introduction to Indigenous ethnobotany through critical literature engagement, work with the regional tribal community, and research and experimentation. While focused on ethnobotany and ethnoecology, this course will also reorient scientists to decolonized methodologies and offer the relational building blocks for further work with interconnected human and more-than-human communities.

title: NAS 332 - Environmental Justice
description: Issues/concerns that led to Executive Order 12898 (environmental policies and conflicts between industries and those seeking environmental protection, including Alaska Native villages, “lower 48” tribes, grassroot community organizations).

title: PHYX 106 - College Physics: Mechanics and Heat
description: Non-Calculus, for science majors. Mechanics, fluids, heat, sound.

title: PHYX 109 - General Physics A: Mechanics
description: Calculus-based, for science/engineering students.

title: STAT 108 - Elementary Statistics
description: Rudimentary data collection, descriptive statistics of univariate and bivariate data, probability, random variables, discrete and continuous distributions. Foundational concepts of confidence intervals and hypothesis tests. Use and abuse of statistics.

title: STAT 109 - Introductory Biostatistics
description: Descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, discrete and continuous distributions, confidence intervals, contingency tests, regression and correlation, tests of hypothesis, analysis of variance. Emphasis: methods and applications used in the biological and natural resource sciences.

title: ZOOL 110 - Introductory Zoology
description: Structure, function, evolution, and diversity of major groups of animals.

title: ZOOL 270 - Human Anatomy
description: Gross and microscopic anatomy of human body. Demonstrations on cadavers; microscopic work. Intended for Kinesiology and Pre-Professional Health students.

title: ANTH 307 - World Heritage and Archaeology
description: Studies of diverse human values and social systems embedded in cultures of a world region through investigations in archaeology and/or cultural anthropology.

title: ANTH 351 - Archaeological Materials Analysis
description: A hands-on introduction to interpreting artifacts from sites. It will cover phases of analysis including: defining problems, attributes, cataloging data, analyzing data, and interpreting results.

title: ANTH 352 - Experimental Archaeology
description: Introduces students to experimental archaeology, or the scientific manner in which archaeologists engage in controlled experiments in order to better understand life in the past.

title: ANTH 353 - Archaeology of Warfare
description: Explore the origins/causes/consequences of warfare. We will gain an understanding of the range of variation in which warfare and human societies have influenced one another.

title: ANTH 354 - Cultural Resource Management
description: Vocationally-oriented introduction to applied archaeology. Ethical, legal, and technical aspects of conserving prehistoric and historic cultural resources of the US.

title: ANTH 357 - Field Archaeology
description: Field experience in local area or in summer field school. Content varies: surface survey, mapping, or excavation. May involve placement as volunteer with federal or state agency.

title: ANTH 358 - Archaeology Lab
description: Archaeology lab activities.

title: ANTH 359 - Special Topics in Archaeology
description: Seminars in selected subfields (concentrations or theory): environmental archaeology, geoarchaeology, archaeoastronomy, zooarchaeology, historical archaeology, ethnohistory. Check with faculty for content.

title: ANTH 395 - Mesoamerican Archaeology
description: Intensive survey of pre-Hispanic cultures of Mexico and Central America. Origins, development, and characteristics of native civilizations: Olmec, Mayan, Teotihuacan, Monte Alban, Toltec, and Aztec.

title: ANTH 340 - Language and Culture
description: Scope and variety of linguistic research. Emphasis on cross-cultural comparison and relation of languages to culture.

title: ANTH 341 - Anthropological Linguistics
description: Introduces formal practice of anthropological linguistics. Structure of human languages; language variation and change; acquisition and meaning. Methodologies include phonetics, phonemics, morphology, and syntax.

title: ANTH 302 - Anthropology of Religion
description: Examine theoretical perspectives and modes of analysis of religious and spiritual traditions with a focus on ritual, magic, and symbolic systems.

title: ANTH 314 - Wicked Problems of the Anthropocene
description: Critically examines relationships between environment, health, and society with a focus on the roles of colonialism, development, and globalization. Organized around several environmental issues including climate change, water pollution and waste, energy, and disasters.

title: ANTH 316 - Anthropology and Development
description: Traditional cultures and their economies. How these societies have adjusted to world economy. Analyze social costs/benefits of economic development.

title: ANTH 317 - Gender and Development
description: Explore how social and economic change happens, focusing on how development policies, programs and issues affect women and men, girls and boys, differently. Examine a range of gender related concerns across the developed and developing world, including: sexual/reproductive health, LGBTQIA+, formal/informal work, education, microfinance, migration, and the environment.

title: ANTH 329 - Selected Topics in Social Anthropology
description: Seminars provide fresh anthropological perspectives on topics such as activism, art, community, environment, film, food, folklore, health, media, medicine, migration, politics.

title: ANTH 390 - World Regions Cultural Seminar
description: Culture, values, and social interaction in cultures of a world region (North America, Latin America, Oceania, Asia, Africa). Analyze cultural integration, contact, change, and development in historical and contemporary contexts.
